title: Schankian Operators Research
excerpt: Four papers on learning semantic operators from data using geometric transformations in complex vector space

children.title: Paper 1: Core Method — Learned Schankian Operators
children.excerpt: Semantic operators can be LEARNED from data rather than hand-coded, using RotatE-style rotations in complex space

children.title: The Problem
children.excerpt: Events like 'cop shot frank' have asymmetric structure. Current approaches treat relations as symmetric.

children.title: Asymmetry in Events
children.excerpt: The cop does something TO frank. Order matters fundamentally.

children.children: Events like 'cop shot frank' have asymmetric structure. The cop does something TO frank — this is fundamentally different from 'frank shot cop'.
This asymmetry is lost in many current approaches that treat relations as symmetric or use simple vector addition.

children.title: Current Approaches Fall Short
children.excerpt: TransE uses addition (symmetric). DistMult is bilinear (can't capture direction).

children.children: Current knowledge graph embedding approaches have limitations:
TransE: h + r ≈ t — addition is commutative, can't capture asymmetry
DistMult: bilinear model, struggles with antisymmetric relations
Most models treat relations as static, not as transformations

children.title: Key Insight: Non-Commutativity
children.excerpt: (agent, operator, patient) ≠ (patient, operator, agent)

children.children: Key insight: (agent, operator, patient) ≠ (patient, operator, agent). Non-commutativity is essential for capturing event semantics.
This is why we need OPERATORS (transformations) rather than simple RELATIONS (static links).

children.title: Our Approach
children.excerpt: Entities as complex vectors, operators as diagonal rotation matrices, RotatE-style scoring

children.title: Entities as Complex Vectors
children.excerpt: Each entity is a d-dimensional complex vector — but what does that really mean?

children.title: What is a Complex Vector?
children.excerpt: Each dimension has two parts: real and imaginary (a + bi)

children.children: Each entity (person, object, concept) is represented as a d-dimensional complex vector.
A 64-dimensional complex vector actually has 128 parameters (64 real + 64 imaginary parts).

children.title: Why Complex Numbers?
children.excerpt: Complex multiplication = rotation. Euler's identity: e^(iθ) = cos(θ) + i·sin(θ)

children.children: Complex numbers naturally represent rotations via Euler's identity:
e^(iθ) = cos(θ) + i·sin(θ)
When you multiply a complex number by e^(iθ), you rotate it by angle θ. This is EXACTLY what we want operators to do — rotate entities in semantic space.

children.title: How Many Dimensions?
children.excerpt: 768 like BERT? Or smaller like RotatE (64-500)?

children.children: You might be used to 768-dimensional vectors from BERT. With complex vectors, we have choices:
Our Phase 1 used 64 complex dimensions (128 params) and achieved 88% operator recovery. Start small, scale up if needed.

children.title: Operators as Rotations
children.excerpt: Each operator is a diagonal rotation matrix in complex space

children.children: Each semantic operator (HARM, TRANSFER, MOVE, etc.) is represented as a diagonal rotation matrix in complex space.
Diagonal means we rotate each dimension independently — simpler than full matrices but still expressive.
This follows RotatE (Sun et al., 2019) but extends it to event semantics rather than static knowledge graphs.

children.title: Scoring Function
children.excerpt: ||agent ⊙ operator - patient|| — how well does the rotation predict the patient?

children.children: Scoring function: ||agent ⊙ operator - patient||
Read as: 'If I rotate the agent by the operator, how close do I get to the patient?'
Lower score = better match. The operator should transform the agent into something close to the patient.

children.title: Training: Margin-Based Ranking
children.excerpt: Learn to score correct triples higher than corrupted ones

children.children: Training uses margin-based ranking loss:
loss = max(0, margin - score_positive + score_negative)
We want correct (agent, op, patient) triples to score LOWER (closer) than corrupted triples where we've swapped in a wrong entity.

children.title: Phase 1 Results
children.excerpt: 88.2% average operator recovery on synthetic data with 5 operators and 20 entities

children.title: Experimental Setup
children.excerpt: 5 ground-truth operators, 20 entities, 5000 training triples

children.children: We created a controlled experiment with known ground truth:
5 ground-truth operators: HARM, TRANSFER, LEARN, MOVE, RECEIVE
20 entities with random initial embeddings (64 complex dimensions each)
5000 synthetic (agent, operator, patient) triples
80/20 train/test split, 200 epochs of training
Key: We KNOW the ground truth operators. The question is: can the model RECOVER them from just seeing examples?

children.title: Ground Truth Operator Design
children.excerpt: Each operator has clear geometric meaning as a rotation pattern

children.children: Each operator is a diagonal rotation matrix with clear semantics:

children.title: The Training Algorithm
children.excerpt: Margin-based ranking loss: score correct triples lower than corrupted ones

children.children: The learner uses margin-based ranking loss:

# For each triple (agent, operator, patient):

# 1. Score correct triple (should be LOW)
score_pos = ||agent ⊙ operator - patient||

# 2. Score corrupted triple (swap agent/patient)
score_neg = ||wrong_agent ⊙ operator - patient||

# 3. Loss: push positive below negative
loss = max(0, margin + score_pos - score_neg)
We never tell the model WHAT the operators are. It discovers them by learning to score correct triples lower than incorrect ones.

children.title: Results: 88.2% Recovery
children.excerpt: All 5 operators recovered with 84-90% fidelity

children.children: ✅ Result: 88.2% average operator recovery. All 5 operators successfully learned from data.
The code: projects/graph-nn/code/schankian_learner_v1.py

children.title: What This Proves
children.excerpt: Operators CAN be learned. Non-commutativity emerges naturally. Ready for real data.

children.children: ✅ Operators CAN be learned from data alone — not hardcoded by humans
✅ Non-commutativity emerges naturally — (agent, op, patient) ≠ (patient, op, agent)
✅ Simple diagonal rotations are sufficient — no need for full matrices
✅ Ranking loss works — no reconstruction term needed
✅ Architecture validated — ready to try real data
This is why Phase 1 matters: it proves the CONCEPT before we invest in messy real-world data. The architecture works.

children.title: Paper 2: Entity State Tracking
children.excerpt: Track how entities evolve through narratives. Frank-before-shooting ≠ Frank-after-shooting.

children.title: State as Trajectory
children.excerpt: Entity state is not a snapshot but a trajectory through vector space — the path taken matters

children.title: Not a Snapshot
children.excerpt: A sequence of vectors, not a single point

children.children: The 'state' of an entity is not a single vector but a trajectory through vector space — a sequence representing the entity at different points in time.
Frank-before-shooting ≠ Frank-after-shooting, even if both are 'Frank'.

children.title: The Path Matters
children.excerpt: How you got there determines meaning

children.children: The path taken to get to a state matters. Two entities might end up at the same vector location, but with very different histories.
State depends on sequence. The implicit actions that preceded a state matter.

children.title: Implicit Preconditions
children.excerpt: 'Marlowe tumbled out of bed' implies: got INTO bed first, which implies: took off shoes

children.title: The Lego Example
children.excerpt: Stepping on Legos barefoot implies getting out of bed implies taking off shoes earlier

children.children: 'Marlowe rolled out of bed and staggered down the dark hallway, forgetting about the Lego blocks. "Dammit!" he screamed, limping the rest of the way to the bathroom.'
Implicit state chain:
Getting into bed → removal of shoes (implicit)
Getting out of bed → still shoeless (implicit)
Stepping on Legos barefoot → pain (implicit, explains the scream)
None of this is stated. All inferred from world knowledge.

children.title: General Principle
children.excerpt: To leave a place, you must first have gone there

children.children: More foundationally: To leave a place, you must first have gone to that place.
Every action has implicit preconditions (what must be true before) and postconditions (what becomes true after).
A couple at the altar in fancy dress → getting married → implies: were dating, someone proposed, someone said yes, will be married couple.

children.title: ⭐ The Big Idea: Implicit-Explicit Document Enrichment
children.excerpt: Annotate ALL implicit state, then use hierarchical summarization for efficient RAG. THIS MAY BE THE MOST IMPACTFUL IDEA.

children.title: The Problem with Current RAG
children.excerpt: RAG retrieves surface text but misses implicit information humans infer automatically

children.children: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems retrieve chunks based on surface similarity. They miss IMPLICIT information.
Document: 'Marlowe tumbled out of bed and stepped on a Lego.'
Query: 'Was Marlowe wearing shoes?'
RAG: 'No mention of shoes.' Human: 'No — he's barefoot because he just got out of bed.'

children.title: The Extreme Case: Hemingway's Six Words
children.excerpt: 'For sale: baby shoes, never worn.' — devastating because of everything LEFT UNSAID

children.children: "For sale: baby shoes, never worn."
This famous six-word story is devastating BECAUSE of everything left unsaid. A RAG system searching for 'grief' or 'child loss' would never find it. But humans instantly infer:
Someone was expecting a baby (shoes purchased in anticipation)
The baby either died, was stillborn, or never came (LOSS)
The shoes were never used (negation of intended PTRANS)
Selling them → processing grief, moving on
The brevity itself → too painful to elaborate

Annotated:
  EVENTS:
    - TRANSFER(?, shoes, seller) — acquired
    - ANTICIPATE(seller, baby) — expected baby
    - LOSS(seller, baby) — baby never came
    - ¬PTRANS(baby, shoes) — never used
  STATES:
    - seller.emotion = grief
    - shoes.condition = new
Six words. More implicit than explicit. Invisible to any surface-level retrieval.

children.title: The Solution: Two-Phase Architecture
children.excerpt: Phase 1: Annotate offline. Phase 2: Hierarchical retrieval.

children.children: Phase 1 (Offline): Annotate documents with ALL implicit state:

Original: 'Marlowe tumbled out of bed'

Annotated:
  [IMPLICIT: Was in bed → shoes removed]
  [IMPLICIT: Now standing, barefoot, near bed]
Phase 2 (Query Time): Hierarchical summarization:
Top: 'Document about Marlowe's morning'
Mid: 'Gets out of bed, steps on Lego'
Detail: Full annotated text

children.title: Why This Matters
children.excerpt: Separates knowledge capture from retrieval efficiency. Could be a standalone paper.

children.children: This may be the most practically impactful idea in this research.
RAG systems currently fail on implicit reasoning
Making implicit explicit is one-time preprocessing
Hierarchy keeps retrieval fast despite bloat
Annotated corpus could train implicit-state models
Separates: (1) capturing knowledge, (2) efficient retrieval
Potential standalone paper: 'Implicit-Explicit Document Enrichment for Retrieval-Augmented Generation'

children.title: Paper 3: Temporal Structure & Entity Chains
children.excerpt: Multiple entities traveling through time = multiple arrows. Handle chronology and coreference.

children.title: The Chronology Challenge
children.excerpt: Non-commutative operators need sequence. Time has one arrow, but multiple entities = a fusillade of arrows

children.children: A Schankian primitive neuron + two entity neurons tells us the operator is relevant. But it doesn't tell us SEQUENCE — critical for non-commutative operators.
Time has one arrow, but multiple entities traveling through time = multiple arrows (a 'fusillade').
We need separate state chains for each entity, with synchronization links between them.

children.title: Entity Identification (Coreference)
children.excerpt: 'The detective' = 'Marlowe'. Can't track state without resolving anaphora.

children.title: The Marlowe Chair Example
children.excerpt: Must resolve: detective = Marlowe; chair = the one he's sitting in; linoleum = same floor

children.children: 'The detective leaned too far back. The chair's feet slid on the linoleum floor. Marlowe grunted as his head clunked against the linoleum.'
Must resolve:
detective = Marlowe (same person)
chair = the one he's sitting in (implicit)
linoleum (first mention) = linoleum (second mention) = same floor

children.title: Why This Matters
children.excerpt: Can't track Frank's state if you don't know 'the detective' IS Frank

children.children: Anaphora resolution feeds directly into state tracking. Can't track Frank's state if you don't know 'the detective' IS Frank.
Entity identification is a prerequisite for entity state tracking. They must be solved together or in sequence.

children.title: Paper 4: Semantic Reward Machines
children.excerpt: Operators as the shared language of understanding and planning — comprehension IS planning in reverse

children.title: Core Thesis: Understanding IS Planning
children.excerpt: The same operators that parse narratives also generate plans. Forward model = planning, inverse = comprehension.

children.children: 🎯 The same Schankian operators that let you UNDERSTAND a narrative also let you PLAN and EXECUTE actions. Comprehension and agency use the same primitives.

'John went to the store and bought milk' — you understand it because you can see the plan: PTRANS(John, home→store) then ATRANS(store, milk, John). An agent planning to get milk constructs the SAME sequence. Same operators, opposite direction.

children.title: Reward Machines: The Missing Bridge
children.excerpt: Schank's scripts ARE Reward Machines — same structure, 45 years apart, different fields

children.children: Reward Machines (RMs) are finite state automata for RL tasks. Transitions triggered by 'propositional events.' Schankian operators ARE those transition functions.
The restaurant script (Schank 1977) = ENTER→SIT→ORDER→EAT→PAY. This IS a valid Reward Machine from the 2022 RL literature. Same structure, independently discovered.

Neural Reward Machines (Dahan et al., 2024) solve symbol grounding with probabilistic Moore machines — learning to map observations to symbols. We do the same for language: map text to operator symbols.

children.title: Comprehension Direction
children.excerpt: Script recognition, expectation generation, anomaly detection, implicit state tracking

children.children: Script recognition: Match event sequences against known operator chains
Expectation: 'Sat down at table' → predict MTRANS(menu) next
Anomaly: 'Sat down and started fighting' → unexpected transition → wrong script
State tracking: RM state encodes everything that's happened

children.title: Planning Direction
children.excerpt: Goal decomposition, failure recovery, multi-entity coordination, transfer learning

children.children: Goal decomposition: 'Get milk' → PTRANS + ATRANS + PTRANS
Failure recovery: ATRANS fails (no money) → backtrack or alternate path
Multi-entity: Parallel RMs with synchronization points
Transfer: Restaurant ≈ Café ≈ Fast food — same topology, different params

children.title: The Architecture Stack
children.excerpt: Operator Detection → State Tracker → Temporal Orchestrator → Reward Machine Layer

children.children: Papers 1-3 solve the symbol grounding problem for the RM layer:
Paper 1: Operator Detection (text → operator triples)
Paper 2: Entity State Tracker (operator transforms on embeddings)
Paper 3: Temporal Orchestrator (parallel chains + coreference)
Paper 4: Reward Machine Layer (comprehension + planning on shared operators)
Open question: How do we discover new scripts/RMs from data? DreamCoder-style library learning (#54) could extract recurring RM topologies from narrative corpora.

children.title: The Big Claims
children.excerpt: Comprehension = planning in reverse. Schank = RL. Finite operators. Built-in interpretability.

children.children: Comprehension and planning are the SAME machinery run in opposite directions
Schank (1977) and RL Reward Machines (2022) independently converged on the same structure
The operator vocabulary is finite, learnable, and compositional — novel situations = novel compositions
Interpretability is built in — every decision expressible as human-readable operator sequences
Transfer across domains is natural — operators are invariant, grounding changes

children.title: 📚 Reading List
children.excerpt: Key papers to read: RotatE, TransE, Schank 1972, ProPara, VerbNet...

children.title: Knowledge Graph Embeddings
children.excerpt: RotatE (your inspiration), TransE, ComplEx, KG Survey

children.title: RotatE (Sun et al., ICLR 2019)
children.excerpt: YOUR DIRECT INSPIRATION — Relations as rotations in complex space

children.children: RotatE: Knowledge Graph Embedding by Relational Rotation in Complex Space
Sun et al., ICLR 2019
YOUR DIRECT INSPIRATION. Defines relations as rotations in complex vector space. You're extending this from static relations to event operators.
DeepGraphLearning/KnowledgeGraphEmbedding

children.title: TransE (Bordes et al., NeurIPS 2013)
children.excerpt: The original KG embedding — h + r ≈ t

children.children: Translating Embeddings for Modeling Multi-relational Data
Bordes et al., NeurIPS 2013
The original knowledge graph embedding. Uses h + r ≈ t (head + relation ≈ tail). Foundational but can't handle asymmetry well.

children.title: ComplEx (Trouillon et al., ICML 2016)
children.excerpt: Complex embeddings for asymmetric relations

children.children: Complex Embeddings for Simple Link Prediction
Trouillon et al., ICML 2016
Introduces complex-valued embeddings to handle asymmetric relations. Predecessor to RotatE.

children.title: KG Embedding Survey (Wang et al., 2017)
children.excerpt: Comprehensive overview of the field

children.children: Knowledge Graph Embedding: A Survey of Approaches and Applications
Wang et al., IEEE TKDE 2017
Comprehensive survey covering TransE, DistMult, ComplEx, and many others. Good for positioning your work.

children.title: Classic NLP: Conceptual Dependency
children.excerpt: Schank 1972, Scripts/Plans/Goals book — what you're reviving

children.title: Schank (1972) — THE ORIGINAL
children.excerpt: Conceptual Dependency: A Theory of Natural Language Understanding

children.children: Conceptual Dependency: A Theory of Natural Language Understanding
Roger Schank, Cognitive Psychology, 1972
THE ORIGINAL primitive acts paper. Introduces ~14 primitives like PTRANS (physical transfer), ATRANS (abstract transfer), PROPEL, etc. You're asking: can these be LEARNED instead of hand-coded?

children.title: Schank & Abelson (1977) — Scripts Book
children.excerpt: Scripts, Plans, Goals and Understanding

children.children: Scripts, Plans, Goals and Understanding: An Inquiry into Human Knowledge Structures
Schank & Abelson, 1977 (Book)
Extends CD theory to scripts (stereotyped event sequences like 'restaurant script'). Relevant to your temporal structure / entity chain work.

children.title: Entity State Tracking
children.excerpt: ProPara, OpenPI — benchmarks for your Paper 2

children.title: ProPara (Dalvi et al., NAACL 2018)
children.excerpt: KEY BENCHMARK — Tracking State Changes in Procedural Text

children.children: Tracking State Changes in Procedural Text: A Challenge Dataset and Models for Process Paragraph Comprehension
Dalvi et al., NAACL 2018
KEY BENCHMARK. Dataset for tracking entity state changes (location, existence) in procedural text. 81k datapoints. Could evaluate your entity state tracking against this.

children.title: OpenPI (Tandon et al., EMNLP 2020)
children.excerpt: Open domain procedural text — WikiHow data

children.children: A Dataset for Tracking Entities in Open Domain Procedural Text
Tandon et al., EMNLP 2020
29,928 state changes from WikiHow. More open-domain than ProPara. Another potential evaluation benchmark.

children.title: Event/Script Learning
children.excerpt: Narrative graphs, script learning — closest to your vision

children.title: Narrative Event Evolutionary Graph (2018)
children.excerpt: Graph-based event representation for script prediction

children.children: Constructing Narrative Event Evolutionary Graph for Script Event Prediction
Li et al., 2018
Graph-based event representation using network embedding. Relevant to your temporal structure ideas.

children.title: VerbNet + Generative Lexicon (2022)
children.excerpt: Hand-crafted subevent semantics — what you're trying to LEARN

children.children: Semantic Representations for NLP Using VerbNet and the Generative Lexicon
Frontiers in AI, 2022
Hand-crafted semantic representations for verbs including subevent structure. This is what you're trying to LEARN from data instead of hand-coding.

children.title: 🧠 Brainstorming
children.excerpt: Far-flung connections, unexpected implications — updated hourly

children.title: Neural Oscillation Patterns
children.excerpt: What if primitives ARE brain frequency patterns?

children.children: Wild Connection — Speculative but intriguing

If Schankian primitives are truly universal, maybe they correspond to distinct neural oscillatory signatures. Our 'rotation in complex space' could be a mathematical analog of phase relationships between neural oscillators.
Gamma oscillations for feature binding → primitives bind entity features?
Theta phase for episodic encoding → operators as phase transitions?
Complex-number representation might be biologically plausible
Gamma oscillations
Theta and memory

children.title: The Frame Problem Connection
children.excerpt: Our implicit state approach as a practical solution

children.children: Philosophical Connection — Potentially significant
The 'implicit state' idea directly connects to the Frame Problem — one of the oldest unsolved problems in AI. How does an intelligent system know what DOESN'T change?

Our approach flips it: Instead of asking 'what didn't change?', we ask 'what MUST be true given what happened?' Getting out of bed → was in bed → removed shoes. We enumerate implicit preconditions/postconditions.
Stanford Encyclopedia
McCarthy & Hayes 1969

children.title: Learning Counterfactuals
children.excerpt: Can operators model 'what if' scenarios?

children.children: Future Direction — Exciting for Paper 4
Counterfactual reasoning: 'If Frank hadn't been shot, he wouldn't have gone to the hospital.'
Operator inverses already exist (r₂ = r̄₁ in RotatE terms)
Counterfactual = apply inverse, then apply alternative operator
'If NOT shot' = invert HARM, then apply alternative sequence
Implication: Learning operators might enable counterfactual reasoning without explicit causal graphs — the geometry encodes causal structure implicitly.
Pearl's Causal Hierarchy
Causal Transformers

children.title: ImplicitState-1K Benchmark
children.excerpt: Turn Marlowe/Lego into a dataset — concrete action item

children.children: Practical Idea — Should do this
Turn the Marlowe/Lego example into a benchmark: 1000 short narratives with annotated implicit states.
Text: 'Marlowe rolled out of bed and stepped on a Lego.'
Implicit: was in bed, took off shoes, now barefoot, Lego on floor near bed, stepping barefoot hurts, now in pain
ATOMIC
Event2Mind
ProPara

children.title: DreamCoder-Style Library Learning
children.excerpt: Wake-sleep loops for operator discovery. Wake = explain events with operators. Sleep = refactor into new primitives. MDL compression gives the right operator count.

children.title: From Flat Arrays to Full Rotation Matrices
children.excerpt: First-principles derivation: graph DB flat arrays → bitvector compression → Bloom filter analogy → bitmask-as-rotation hierarchy → diagonal fails for feature interactions → ARCHITECTURE DECISION: full rotation matrices on concatenated participant embeddings.

children.title: Rate-Distortion Bounds on Compression
children.excerpt: Shannon theory gives minimum embedding dims per operator. If R(D) ≈ N, compression provably fails — falsifiability for the entire framework.

children.title: Operators as Reward Machine Transitions
children.excerpt: Schankian operators ARE the transition functions of semantic reward machines. Restaurant script = RM where ENTER/ORDER/EAT/PAY trigger state transitions. Neural RMs solve symbol grounding = our operator classification. Bridges NLU and RL planning.

children.label: 58: Delta-Guided Attention
children.excerpt: SAE feature deltas as attention queries for goal-directed disambiguation

children.label: 59: Modal Operators as Mental Primitives
children.excerpt: MBUILD/MTRANS as differentiable modal operators with learnable Kripke accessibility relations — implements Theory of Mind as geometric operator composition

children.label: 60: Goto Considered Helpful
children.excerpt: Two layers of Schankian operators (text-level and process-level) — stack-based backtracking for disambiguation via GLR parsing in the residual stream

children.label: 61: Successor Representation as Script
children.excerpt: Schankian operators ARE the transition matrix T that generates the successor representation — scripts are factored predictive maps with O(K×d²) compression

children.label: 63: GC in the Residual Stream
children.excerpt: Attention heads perform implicit reference counting — erasure heads are neural free(). Script boundaries trigger GC of entity representations.

children.title: 65: PrimeNet Convergence
children.excerpt: PrimeNet (2024) independently validates primitive-based commonsense architecture. Symbolic-geometric bridge: their static labels + our dynamic operators = complete pipeline.

children.title: 70: Spectral Wavelet Decomposition
children.excerpt: Operators at a characteristic spectral scale in event graphs. Graph wavelets find the frequency band where surface verbs converge to primitives.

children.title: 71: Operators as Gauge Connections
children.excerpt: Operators as gauge connections on a semantic manifold — parallel transport of entity meaning across narrative context. Curvature = narrative surprise. Gauge invariance = cross-linguistic universality.

children.name: 74: Attn Heads as Modal Operators
children.description: Kripke accessibility for KG reasoning. Unifies epistemic/deontic/temporal heads with Bloom filters, gate linearity, Event Calculus.

children.title: 86. Concept Bottleneck Operators
children.excerpt: CBMs force predictions through operator labels — bottleneck leakage measures sufficiency

children.name: 90: Gärdenfors Bridge
children.description: Primitives as dimensions in conceptual space

children.name: 93: Supersaturation as Operator Signature
children.excerpt: Crystal symmetry groups for Schankian primitives via dendritic diffusion supersaturation fingerprints

children.title: #78: State Forking as Script Branching
children.excerpt: Dendritic Mamba state-forking = Schankian script exception handling

children.title: Entry 97: Structural Factoring — Finite Grammar as Cognitive Constraint
children.excerpt: Monk stiff fingers / Beckett French for LMs — finite structural module + infinite semantic backbone

children.title: Entry 81/98: Grammar Tags as Protocol Layers
children.excerpt: Grammar tags are symbolic protocols for module communication. Structural head speaks grammar; content model listens. Three protocol layers: Surface (syntax), Semantic (operators), Pragmatic (discourse).

children.name: Entry 82: Operators as Compression Codebook for Memory Consolidation

children.label: #77: Recursive Midpoint Diffusion
children.description: Brownian lightning for text generation — recursive midpoint displacement + SAE feature hierarchy. O(log n), embarrassingly parallel, MapReduce-friendly.

children.label: #78: Operator Decomposition Trees
children.description: Schankian operators form recursive decomposition trees (HARM → PROPEL → MOVE → DAMAGE). Combined with NEKG entity tracking and recursive midpoint generation, the tree IS the story plan. Bridges 1970s Conceptual Dependency with modern hierarchical text generation.
